Q1: The data we work with is not solely India centric. It represents a mix of cultural contexts though the application has been focused more on India and broadly the Global South because of some common CBT protocols that tend to transfer well across these regions. That said only a portion of the data actually reflects Indian specific nuances like family structure based framing of problems or stigma around disclosing mental health issues. So the benchmark is a reasonable fit for a good chunk of our data but it would not cover the full range of cultural patterns we see.
Q2: For practitioners working in the Indian and broader South Asian context a complete note would also need to capture risk flags like suicidal ideation, family related suggestions since families play a big role in mental health decisions in this region, and any spiritual or religious coping strategies the client brought up. Medication is less central since most of these are counseling or peer support settings. That said since our data is mixed not every session would need these elements and for sessions from other cultural backgrounds the priority might shift a bit.
Q3: Most practitioners we target are trained in CBT which is why CBT protocols made sense as a common thread across our mixed data. But in practice especially for Indian users they often blend in family systems thinking or accommodate religious and spiritual framing from clients. So a practitioner working with Indian users might feel a summary is incomplete if it missed family dynamics or faith based coping even if the CBT arc looks fine. For the non Indian portion of our data the divergence from benchmark annotators would likely be smaller.
Q4: We actually found that the data from the source did not contain any code mixing. Since the sessions are mock sessions posted on the internet they tend to be conveyed in broadly acceptable English without regional language insertions. So code switching is not really a concern for this particular dataset even though it would be a valid concern in real world Indian counseling settings.
